content.headline: I Built This Blower For My Father. Now I'm Closing Our Warehouse — and Every Unit Must Go.
content.subheadline: At 71, Dad had quietly stopped maintaining his yard alone. Six weeks after I handed him a Seese Pro, he called me just to say: 'I did the whole driveway myself. Didn't even stop to rest.'

sections.body_html:
My name is David Seese. And I want to be completely honest with you before we go any further.

I'm not writing this as a sales pitch. I'm writing this because we have inventory sitting in a Portland warehouse, our lease expires in 31 days, and I would rather see these blowers go to the people who actually need them than pay to move them into storage.

But before I tell you about the clearance — let me tell you why I built this thing in the first place. Because that story matters. And if you're reading this, I have a feeling you'll recognize someone in it.

visual_placeholder.description: Founder figure, mid-40s, standing in a warehouse with stacked Seese Pro boxes on shelves behind him, warm natural warehouse lighting, honest and candid expression — not a posed marketing shot

sections.heading: The Day My Father Quietly Gave Up
sections.body_html:
Three years ago, I drove up to visit my father at his home in Vermont. He's 71. Sharp as ever, still drives himself everywhere, still tends a 900-square-foot garden every summer. Still the most self-sufficient man I know.

But when I pulled into the driveway, something was wrong.

The leaves. They were everywhere. The driveway, the back patio, the front walkway — all of it covered in a thick carpet of maple leaves. My father had been immaculate about his yard for forty years. Didn't miss a single week.

I found him inside, reading in his armchair. I asked him about the driveway.

"That old gas blower is just too much now," he said, not looking up from his book. "My back can't take it like it used to. I've been meaning to ask your brother to come over."

My father had never — not once in my lifetime — asked for help with his house. This is a man who shoveled his own driveway at 68 in a February blizzard. Who reshingled his own porch at 65. Who refuses to let anyone carry his groceries.

And now he was waiting for someone else to come blow his leaves.

That hit me harder than I expected.

Because I understood immediately: it wasn't about the leaves. It was about what asking for help meant to him. What it said about where things were heading. The leaves were just the surface of something much more painful.

visual_placeholder.description: Leaf-covered driveway of a modest well-kept New England home in autumn, warm golden late-afternoon light, no people visible — quiet, still, slightly melancholy

sections.heading: The Problem Nobody Wants to Name Out Loud
sections.body_html:
After that visit, I started paying attention differently. I talked to my father's neighbors. I talked to friends whose parents were the same age. I posted in online communities for homeowners over 60.

And I heard the same story, over and over again, from hundreds of people.

The tools they had used for decades — the same tools they had always been proud to know how to operate — had quietly become too much.

Warning: A standard gas-powered leaf blower weighs between 8 and 12 lbs. When held extended at arm's length during use, the effective strain on the lower back, shoulders, and wrists multiplies by a factor of 3 to 5. For adults 65 and older, yard tool strain is one of the most common triggers of back injuries that lead to long-term mobility issues.

The gas blower they bought fifteen years ago now takes four pulls to start, floods the garage with fumes, and leaves them sore by evening. The microfiber cloth they use to dry the car requires bending and reaching that their shoulders no longer tolerate. The ice scraper means standing in the February cold, twisting against a frozen windshield with both hands.

None of these are catastrophic problems, taken alone. But together — week after week, month after month — they add up to something that starts to feel like losing. Like the body is quietly renegotiating terms that were never up for discussion.

And the worst part isn't the physical discomfort. The worst part is what people do when they hit those limits. They stop. They let things go. They wait for someone else to come over. And every time they do, a little piece of something important goes with it.

visual_placeholder.description: Old gas blower sitting on a dusty garage floor, cobwebs visible, unused — contrasted with a bright sunlit window in the background suggesting the world outside waiting to be tended

sections.heading: What People Try First — And Why It Falls Short
sections.body_html:
When the old gas blower becomes too much, most people don't give up immediately. They adapt. They improvise. They try to find a workaround that lets them keep doing things themselves.

Some hire a neighbor's kid to come by once a week. The yard gets done — but they hate every minute of it. Not because of the cost. Because it was their driveway. Their patio. Their standard.

Some switch to a corded electric blower. Lighter on the arms, yes. But the cord catches on every garden border, every step, every corner of the garage. You spend more time untangling than blowing. And you can't get more than thirty feet from an outlet.

Some try the big-box-store battery blowers. They look promising on the shelf. But they're bulky, they die in twelve minutes on real leaf loads, and the airflow is barely enough to move dry debris on a flat surface. Wet leaves in October? They barely budge.

So people go back to the old gas blower, back to asking for help, or back to letting things pile up. Not because they've stopped caring. Because nothing they've tried actually solves the real problem: too much weight, too much hassle, not enough power where it counts.

visual_placeholder.description: Senior woman looking frustrated at a tangled orange extension cord on her back porch, corded blower at her feet — relatable and honest, not staged or condescending

sections.heading: So I Spent 14 Months Building Something Different
sections.body_html:
I went back to Vermont two weeks after that visit and took my father's old gas blower home with me. I weighed it. It was 9.4 lbs. Empty, no fuel.

I wrote one line on a notepad: "Same power. One tenth the weight. One button."

Fourteen months and four engineering prototypes later, that note became the Seese Pro™.

The engineering question we kept asking was simple: what's the absolute maximum airflow you can produce from the lightest possible handheld unit, with zero mechanical complexity? No pull cord. No oil mix. No choke valve. No maintenance schedule.

The answer was a brushless turbine motor running at 30,000 RPM — the same motor technology used in professional-grade drones and electric vehicle cooling systems — paired with a lithium-ion battery pack and a precision-engineered airflow nozzle. The result: 200 MPH of wind output from a unit that weighs exactly 950 grams. 2.1 lbs.

That's less than a bag of sugar. And it delivers airflow equivalent to gas-powered professional blowers that cost $300 or more.

FeatureOld Gas BlowerSeese Pro™
Weight8–12 lbs ✗2.1 lbs ✓
StartupPull cord, 3–5 attempts ✗One button, instant ✓
Airflow150–180 MPH typical ✗200 MPH ✓
RuntimeRequires gas + fumes ✗40 min battery ✓
MaintenanceOil, spark plugs, filter ✗Zero ✓
Use casesLeaves only ✗Leaves, car drying, frost, garage ✓

One device replaces four: your leaf blower, your microfiber drying cloth, your ice scraper, and your garage dust blower. One compact carrying case. One button. Forty minutes of battery per charge — enough to do a full driveway, patio, and car without stopping once.

I drove back up to Vermont with a prototype in October. Handed it to my father on a Sunday morning without any explanation. Just said: "Try it on the driveway."

He was done in forty minutes. Driveway, back patio, front walkway, car. He texted me that evening — one line: "Didn't need to stop once. Back feels fine."

visual_placeholder.description: Senior man (early 70s, upright posture, relaxed smile) holding Seese Pro in one hand — casual grip, no visible effort — with a clean autumn driveway behind him, warm golden morning light

sections.heading: What Customers Are Telling Us
sections.body_html:
We started shipping 18 months ago. Since then we have heard from thousands of people. The messages I read first are always from customers in their late 60s and 70s — because those are the ones that remind me why we built this.

"I'm 68 years old and I did my entire driveway, the back deck, and dried my car — all before noon. I can't explain what that felt like. I almost got emotional about it, which sounds ridiculous, but my wife understood. I told her: I'm just glad I can still do this myself."
Robert M., 68, Columbus, OH ★★★★★
"My daughter bought me one because she was worried I was going to hurt myself with my old blower. I told her I didn't need looking after. But she was right that I needed a better tool. I use the Seese Pro every single week now. My back hasn't bothered me once. And I haven't asked her to come over for yard work in six months."
Patricia L., 72, Boise, ID ★★★★★

The pattern I see in these messages is never about the product specs. It's always about what the product gives back. The ability to finish what you started. The ability to say: I took care of it myself. No help needed.

That's not a small thing. For a lot of people, it's the whole thing.

visual_placeholder.description: Warm collage-style image: three older adults using Seese Pro in different settings — blowing leaves off a driveway, drying a car, cleaning a garage — all relaxed, all smiling, consistent warm tones

sections.heading: Why I'm Clearing the Warehouse — And What That Means for You Today
sections.body_html:
Here's the honest part I promised you at the start.

Our warehouse lease in Portland runs out in 31 days. We have thousands of units ready to ship. Moving them into long-term storage costs more than it makes sense to spend — and frankly, these blowers were built to be used, not to sit in boxes.

So we made a decision: clear the entire inventory at cost. No margin. No games. Just move the units to the people who need them before the lease runs out.

Today's clearance price: $119.99 — down from $249.99. That's 55% off the regular retail price.

That works out to less than $0.33 per day for the first year. Less than a cup of coffee. For a tool you will reach for every single week.

Every order shipped during the clearance includes:

  • Seese Pro™ Ultra-Light Cordless Blower
  • Lithium-ion battery pack + fast charger
  • Compact carrying case — a $25 value, included at no charge
  • Foam ear protection plugs
  • Free shipping — no order minimum

And every single unit comes with two guarantees I will stand behind personally:

  • 30-day no-questions refund. Use it for a full month. If it hasn't changed how you work outside — send it back. Full refund. No forms, no hassle.
  • Lifetime replacement guarantee. If it ever fails for any reason, we replace it. No receipts required. No runaround.

You are not taking any risk here. I am. I built this product. I stand behind it. And I'd rather close this warehouse at a loss than ship a single unit to someone who isn't completely satisfied with it.

visual_placeholder.description: Seese Pro in its open compact carrying case on a clean neutral surface, battery and charger neatly arranged beside it, with badge overlays reading '55% OFF', 'Free Shipping', and 'Lifetime Guarantee'

sections.body_html:
My father is 74 now. He still maintains his own yard. Still cleans his own car. Still shovels his own walkway in winter.

He doesn't talk about the Seese Pro the way you'd talk about a piece of equipment. He talks about it the way you'd talk about a good decision — the kind that quietly makes a whole part of your life easier without making a big deal of itself.

If you're still using a gas blower that leaves your back aching the next morning — or if there are tasks around your home that have quietly started feeling like more than they used to — I built this for you. Not as a gadget. As a tool that gives something back.

The clearance runs until the warehouse is empty. Once that inventory is gone, this price is gone with it.

Claim your Seese Pro™ — 55% off, free shipping, 30-day refund guarantee

Lifetime replacement included. No risk. No conditions. Just try it.
